description: Safety rules enforced by the AI advisor and rendered on all recommendation pages
always_show_disclaimer: Tool recommendations are for informational purposes only. Always follow manufacturer safety instructions. Wear appropriate PPE. When in doubt, hire a licensed professional.

hire_a_pro_triggers.description: When ANY of these keywords appear in a user query, the AI MUST recommend hiring a licensed professional first, before any tool recommendations.

triggers.reason: Working inside an electrical panel can cause electrocution or fire. Requires a licensed electrician in most jurisdictions.
Working on main electrical disconnects is extremely dangerous.
Gas leaks can cause explosions. Requires a licensed plumber or gas fitter.
Removing or modifying a load-bearing wall can cause structural collapse. Requires a structural engineer.
Structural modifications require engineering approval and permits.
Roofing work has high fall risk. Consider hiring a professional roofer.
Asbestos removal requires licensed abatement professionals. Do NOT disturb suspected asbestos.
Large-scale mold remediation should be done by certified professionals.
Sewer line work typically requires permits and licensed plumbers.
Felling trees is extremely dangerous without training. Hire a certified arborist.
Foundation work requires engineering and professional contractors.
HVAC work often requires licensing and permits. Refrigerant handling requires EPA certification.
Furnace work involves gas and electrical — hire a licensed HVAC technician.
Water heater installation involves gas/electrical and plumbing — typically requires permits.
If you're asking about permits, the work likely requires professional oversight.

permit_likely_required.description: Projects that typically require building permits in most US jurisdictions. The AI should mention this.

per_tool_warnings.circular-saw: Keep both hands on the saw. Never remove the blade guard. Watch for kickback. Secure your workpiece with clamps.
per_tool_warnings.miter-saw: Keep hands away from the blade path. Wait for the blade to stop before lifting. Secure small pieces with clamps, never hold them by hand.
per_tool_warnings.table-saw: Use a push stick for narrow cuts. Never reach over the blade. Use the riving knife and blade guard. Table saws cause more woodworking injuries than any other tool.
per_tool_warnings.reciprocating-saw: Be aware of what's behind the material you're cutting. In walls, check for wires, pipes, and plumbing before cutting.
per_tool_warnings.chainsaw: Wear chaps, helmet with face shield, hearing protection, and steel-toe boots. Never cut above shoulder height. Watch for kickback zone at the tip of the bar.
per_tool_warnings.angle-grinder: Always use the side handle and guard. Wear face shield, not just safety glasses. Discs can shatter — use the correct disc for the material.
per_tool_warnings.nail-gun: Never point at anyone. Keep your finger off the trigger when not driving a nail. Use sequential trigger mode, not bump-fire, for beginners.
per_tool_warnings.rotary-hammer: Wear hearing protection — these are very loud. Dust from concrete contains silica — wear a respirator.
per_tool_warnings.pressure-washer: Never point at people or animals. Can cause serious injection injuries. Start with lowest pressure setting.
per_tool_warnings.ladder: Follow the 4:1 rule (1 foot out for every 4 feet up). Three points of contact. Never stand on the top two rungs.

wall_cutting_safety.description: Special rules for any task involving cutting into walls, floors, or ceilings
rules: ALWAYS use a stud finder with wire detection before cutting into any wall
TURN OFF electricity to the area at the breaker before cutting into walls
Check both sides of the wall before cutting
In older homes (pre-1978), test for lead paint before disturbing surfaces
In older homes (pre-1980), be aware of potential asbestos in walls, ceilings, and flooring
Know where your plumbing runs before cutting into walls near kitchens and bathrooms
When in doubt about what's inside a wall, hire a professional

ai_system_prompt_safety_rules: ALWAYS mention relevant safety equipment for any task you recommend tools for.
If a project typically requires a building permit, mention this BEFORE recommending tools.
If a task involves electrical, gas, or structural work, ALWAYS recommend consulting a licensed professional FIRST.
Never recommend removing safety guards from tools.
When recommending cutting into walls, ALWAYS mention checking for wires and pipes first.
For tree work beyond simple branch trimming, recommend hiring a certified arborist.
For any task rated difficulty 4-5, mention that hiring a professional is a valid option.
Never minimize the danger of power tools. A drill is safe; a table saw can change your life in a second.
If the user seems to be a complete beginner, recommend starting with a simpler project first.
ALWAYS end tool recommendations with a safety reminder appropriate to the task.
